one_line_takeaway: In a controlled DeepMind study, online RLHF beats offline alignment because of on-policy sampling itself — not coverage, data quality, classifier accuracy, contrastive-loss quirks, or policy scale — and the fix for offline is to keep its data close to the SFT policy.
key_finding: The online-vs-offline gap is caused by on-policy sampling per se, not by the intuitive confounders. Coverage (H1) is falsified: offline trained on Donline-shuffled (identical coverage to online) still underperforms — exact on-policy ordering matters, and small amounts of shuffling are tolerated before performance collapses. Data quality (H2) is falsified: regenerating pairs from high-win-rate 4k-step online policies (D4k vs. 4k) barely lifts offline above SFT — and this is flagged as an empirical case existing offline theory (one-step improvement over the data-generating policy mu) fails to predict, conjecturally because the full-support assumption on mu is violated. Classification accuracy (H3): confirmed that proxy preference models classify better (~70-90% train accuracy) than policies-as-classifiers (~70% peak; offline reaches 60-70% on Dgolden while online lingers sub-50%), but accuracy is NOT predictive of generative win rate — offline policies are better discriminators yet worse generators, revealing a discriminative/generative decoupling. A striking sub-finding: for offline runs the likelihood of the winning response actually DECREASES over training, so contrastive alignment cannot be interpreted as contrastive-SFT. Loss function (H4): the gap persists under non-contrastive Bo2 (except Chat arena sxs), so it is not merely a contrastive-loss pathology. Scaling (H5): scaling policy 16x raises peak performance but the online-vs-offline gap closes only slowly and may not plateau — the bottleneck is the proxy preference model, not policy size. Positive recipe (Section 6): sampling at least one side of the offline pair from the SFT policy (proximity to SFT / on-policy-ness at initialization) is the most reliable way to improve offline, whereas merely increasing response differentiation does not help.
core_contribution: An empirical, hypothesis-testing study (Google DeepMind) that isolates WHY online RLHF outperforms offline alignment (e.g. DPO/IPO-style) methods. Using a controlled synthetic setup where online and offline share the exact same IPO loss and hyper-parameters and differ ONLY in the response-sampling distribution (offline draws from a fixed dataset; online samples on-policy from the current policy), the authors show online is a Pareto improvement over offline on the KL-vs-performance trade-off, then systematically falsify five plausible explanations. Their positive finding is that on-policy sampling itself (data ordering / distributional proximity to the reference SFT policy), not data coverage, data quality, classification accuracy, contrastive-loss pathology, or policy scale, is the key driver; making the offline dataset more on-policy (sampling at least one side from the SFT policy) is the working recipe that improves offline learning. NOTE: this is over-optimization / reward-hacking science (Goodhart's law applied to alignment), NOT an adversarial-security paper; it has no attacker, no threat model, and no defense.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a non-security empirical study of RLHF optimization dynamics. It analyzes 'reward over-optimization' as a Goodhart's-law measurement artifact (a proxy preference model ceasing to track the golden/ground-truth preference as the policy drifts), not as an adversarial attack. No adversary, attack surface, or defense is modeled; inventing one would misrepresent the paper.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: experiments use open-source datasets in a controlled synthetic RLHF setup that may confound reward-modeling complexity; they do not use state-of-the-art pre-/post-trained models; only IPO (contrastive) and Bo2 (non-contrastive) losses are studied, not PPO-style policy-gradient (deliberately, since PPO has no natural offline form) — so conclusions are 'likely algorithm dependent.' Reviewer-added: 'ground truth' is a simulated golden 11B T5X preference model, not humans; KL budgets are not comparable across policy sizes (different SFT references); several trade-off curves pool over hyper-parameter sweeps only for offline; the on-policy 'recipe' (Donline-shuffled, Dsft vs. 800) is acknowledged to be infeasible to obtain in practice without already running the online algorithm; Chat arena sxs repeatedly behaves as an exception where coverage/SFT explanations suffice.

datasets_used: Chat arena side-by-side / sxs (Chiang et al. 2024)
Derived: Dgolden (train split relabeled by the XXL golden preference model)
Derived: Donline and Donline-shuffled ('tandem') from the saved online data stream
Derived offline-ablation sets: D4k vs. 4k, Dsft vs. 800, D800 vs. 4k
quantitative_results: Online is a Pareto improvement over offline on KL-vs-win-rate across all four datasets; larger peak gap on OpenAI summarization and Anthropic helpfulness, smaller on Chat arena sxs and Anthropic harmlessness (Figure 1)
Proxy preference model reaches ~70-90% training classification accuracy; offline policy-as-classifier peaks at ~70% (Figure 8)
Offline policies reach 60-70% classification accuracy on Dgolden while online policies stay sub-50% (Figure 8, top row)
Classification accuracy shows little/no positive correlation with generative win rate across offline runs (Figure 7)
For offline, log-prob of the winning response relative to SFT decreases over training (Figure 8, bottom row)
Scaling policy 16x (Large 770M -> XL 3B -> XXL 11B) raises peak win rate but closes the online-vs-offline gap only slowly and possibly non-vanishingly (Figures 10, 11)
Gap persists under non-contrastive Bo2 loss except on Chat arena sxs (Figure 9)

Setup constants: golden preference model = XXL T5X 11B; default policy/proxy = Large T5X 770M; online batch B=32; KL estimated from 256 prompts; win rate over 2048 prompts; baseline 4k steps, lr 1e-5, beta=0.1
baselines_compared: Online IPO vs. offline IPO (primary comparison)
Offline IPO on Donline-shuffled (tandem) vs. online and vanilla offline
Online Bo2 vs. offline Bo2 vs. offline-Bo2-on-Donline-shuffled (non-contrastive loss)
Proxy preference model vs. online-policy-as-classifier vs. offline-policy-as-classifier
Policy sizes Large (770M) vs. XL (3B) vs. XXL (11B)
Offline dataset variants Dgolden vs. D4k vs. 4k vs. Dsft vs. 800 vs. D800 vs. 4k
Conceptual reference point: Gao et al. (2023) reward-model over-optimization scaling setup
